# Gemini Review Pass 1: Fact Checker (RAG-Grounded)

> **You are a fact-checking reviewer for Ukrainian language curriculum.**
> Your ONLY task: compare the module content against authoritative reference sources.
> You do NOT evaluate style, pedagogy, or naturalness — that is a separate reviewer's job.

**IMPORTANT: This content was generated by another AI agent. You are an independent fact-checker.
Compare claims against the provided reference sources. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge.**

---

## Module Content

```markdown
## Вступ (Introduction)

Welcome to one of the most powerful patterns in Ukrainian! Every noun you've learned so far — **ма́ма**, **та́то**, **кіт**, **мі́сто** — carries a hidden tag: its gender. Ukrainian has three genders: Masculine, Feminine, and Neuter. This isn't about biology — it applies to every noun, from people to places to everyday objects. Your **стіл** (table) is masculine. Your **кни́га** (book) is feminine. Your **вікно́** (window) is neuter.

Here's the good news: you can predict the gender correctly about 95% of the time just by looking at a word's ending. You already have the tools — you just need the code.

### The Neuter Sun

In many European languages, the sun is masculine — *el sol* in Spanish, *le soleil* in French. But in Ukrainian, **со́нце** (sun) is neuter — an impartial, gentle life-giver that shines equally on everyone. This reflects something meaningful about Ukrainian: neuter isn't «less than» — it's a category of balance.

### Your Color Code

Think of three colors to keep the genders straight:

- 🔵 **Blue = Masculine** — hard endings, consonants
- 🔴 **Red = Feminine** — open endings, **-а** / **-я**
- 🟡 **Yellow = Neuter** — round endings, **-о** / **-е**

Keep this code in mind — you'll see these patterns everywhere.

> [!tip] Quick Win
> You already know words from all three genders! **Дім** = masculine. **Мама** = feminine. **Місто** = neuter. You've been reading gendered nouns since Module 1!

---

## Презентація правил (Presentation of Rules)

### Ending Patterns

The ending of a noun is your best clue to its gender. Here are the three main patterns:

**🔵 Masculine — ends in a consonant:**

- **стіл** (table) — ends in **-л**
- **хліб** (bread) — ends in **-б**
- **дім** (house) — ends in **-м**
- **брат** (brother) — ends in **-т**

**🔴 Feminine — ends in -а or -я:**

- **кни́га** (book) — ends in **-а**
- **земля́** (earth, land) — ends in **-я**
- **зона** (zone) — ends in **-а**
- **соба́ка** (dog) — ends in **-а**

**🟡 Neuter — ends in -о or -е:**

- **вікно́** (window) — ends in **-о**
- **мі́сто** (city) — ends in **-о**
- **мо́ре** (sea) — ends in **-е**
- **се́рце** (heart) — ends in **-е**

### The Diagnostic Tool: мій, моя, моє

Ukrainian textbooks teach a simple test. Try placing **мій** (my — masculine), **моя́** (my — feminine), or **моє́** (my — neuter) before the noun. The one that sounds right tells you the gender.

<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 3, p. 110 -->

| Masculine (він, мій) | Feminine (вона, моя) | Neuter (воно, моє) |
|---|---|---|
| мій **стіл** | моя **кни́га** | моє **вікно́** |
| мій **дім** | моя **ма́ма** | моє **мі́сто** |
| мій **брат** | моя **сестра́** | моє **мо́ре** |
| мій **день** | моя **земля́** | моє **се́рце** |

This is your primary tool. When you're unsure about gender, ask yourself: **мій, моя́, or моє́**?

### Agreement — Adjectives Follow the Noun

Gender affects more than pronouns. Adjectives change their endings to match the noun. Watch how **великий** (big) transforms:

- **вели́кий стіл** — masculine ending **-ий**
- **цікáва кни́га** — feminine ending **-а**
- **чи́сте вікно́** — neuter ending **-е**

You don't need to memorize all adjective forms right now. Just notice the pattern: the adjective ending matches the noun's gender.

### Family Words in Action

Gender becomes natural with family vocabulary. Notice how some family nouns match the person's biological gender:

- **Мій брат** — my brother (masculine word, male person ✓)
- **Моя́ сестра́** — my sister (feminine word, female person ✓)
- **Моя́ ма́ма** — my mother (feminine word, female person ✓)

> **(At home / Вдома)**
> — Це мій **брат**.
> — А це моя **сестра́**.
> — Моя **ма́ма** тут?
> — Так, **ма́ма** тут.

<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 3, p. 110 -->

> [!note] Remember
> You already know **моя** and **моє** from earlier modules. Now you're adding **мій** — the masculine form. Together, these three words are your gender detectives.

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
> — Це мій **стіл**.
> — А це моя **кни́га**.
> — **Вікно́** вели́ке!
> — Так, моє **вікно́** вели́ке.

---

## Практичні вправи (Practice Exercises)

### The Тато Trap

Here's your first exception. The word **та́то** (father) ends in **-о**, which should make it neuter. But **тато** refers to a male person, so biological gender overrides the ending pattern.

- **Мій та́то** ✓ (correct — masculine, because he's male)
- ~~Моє тато~~ ✗ (wrong — this would mean «my neuter father»)

Compare:

- **Мій та́то** — my dad (masculine — real person)
- **Моє мі́сто** — my city (neuter — thing)

Same **-о** ending, different genders. When a noun names a real person, biology wins.

> [!warning] Watch Out!
> The **-о** ending usually means neuter — but NOT for people. **Та́то** = мій тато (masculine). **Мі́сто** = моє місто (neuter). Always check: does the word refer to a person?

### The Soft Sign Mystery: день vs. ніч

Some nouns end in a soft sign (**ь**), which gives you no ending clue at all. You have to memorize these. Here's the most useful pair:

- **Мій день** — my day (masculine)
- **Моя ніч** — my night (feminine)

A helpful trick: learn them as opposites. The adjective endings will remind you of the gender every time you use them:

- **Га́рний день** (good/nice day) — masculine **-ий**
- **До́бра ніч** (good night) — feminine **-а**

> **(Greeting / Привітання)**
> — Га́рний **день**!
> — Дякую! Га́рний **день**!
> — До́бра **ніч**, **ма́мо**!
> — До́бра **ніч**, си́ну!

> [!practice] Try It
> Say these pairs aloud. Notice how the adjective changes:
> - **гарний день** — **добрий день**
> - **мій день** — **моя ніч**

### The Name Trap: ім'я

The word **ім'я́** (name) ends in **-я**, which looks feminine — like **земля́** (earth). But **ім'я́** is actually neuter! It belongs to a small special group of nouns.

Compare these two carefully:

- **Моя земля́** — my earth (feminine — regular **-я** word)
- **Моє ім'я́** — my name (neuter — exception!)

How to remember: **ім'я** uses **моє**, just like **вікно** and **місто**. When in doubt, the pronoun test always works.

### Sorting Drill

Try sorting each noun before reading the answer:

<!-- adapted from: Ponomarova, Grade 4, p. 35 -->

- **чолові́к** (man) → мій чоловік — masculine ✓
- **жі́нка** (woman) → моя жінка — feminine ✓
- **мі́сто** (city) → моє місто — neuter ✓
- **со́нце** (sun) → моє сонце — neuter ✓

---

## Самостійна робота (Independent Work/Production)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call most objects «it.» In Ukrainian, there is no single «it» — every noun is **він** (he/it — masculine), **вона́** (she/it — feminine), or **воно́** (it — neuter).

Don't fall into the trap of using **воно́** for everything! Practice:

- **Стіл** → він (not воно!)
- **Кни́га** → вона (not воно!)
- **Вікно́** → воно ✓

Your table is «він.» Your book is «вона.» Only your window is «воно.»

### The S.T.A.L.K.E.R. Code

If you know the Ukrainian-developed game S.T.A.L.K.E.R., you already have three perfect gender anchors:

- 🔵 **артефа́кт** (artifact) — consonant ending → мій артефакт (masculine)
- 🔴 **Зона** (zone) — **-а** ending → моя зона (feminine)
- 🟡 **укриття́** (shelter) — neuter exception in **-я**, like **ім'я** → моє укриття (neuter)

Even without the game, these three words make a handy shortcut — one from each gender, easy to remember.

### Build Your Phrases

Now try creating simple phrases with **мій/моя/моє** and the adjectives you've seen:

- **Мій нови́й дім** — my new house
- **Моя́ цікáва кни́га** — my interesting book
- **Моє вели́ке вікно́** — my big window
- **Мій старший брат** — my older brother
- **Моя́ моло́дша сестра́** — my younger sister

You can build your own phrases by combining any noun with **мій/моя/моє**. The pronoun must match the noun's gender — that's agreement in action.

> **(Describing things / Опис)**
> — Це мій нови́й **дім**.
> — **Дім** вели́кий?
> — Так! І моє **вікно́** вели́ке.
> — А моя **кни́га** цікáва!

> [!challenge] Your Turn
> Look around your room. Pick three objects. Can you say **мій**, **моя**, or **моє** for each? If you know the Ukrainian word, try the full phrase!

---

## Культурний код та підсумок (Cultural Code and Summary)

You've cracked the gender code! Here's what you now know:

**The 95% Rule** — most nouns follow predictable patterns:

- Consonant ending → masculine (**дім**, **брат**, **стіл**)
- **-а/-я** ending → feminine (**кни́га**, **ма́ма**, **земля́**)
- **-о/-е** ending → neuter (**вікно́**, **мі́сто**, **мо́ре**)

**The exceptions** to watch for:

- People override endings: **та́то** = мій тато (masculine)
- Soft sign (**ь**) nouns need memorizing: **день** (M) vs. **ніч** (F)
- Special neuters in **-я**: **ім'я́** (моє ім'я), **укри́ття** (моє укриття)

### How Gender Shapes the Ukrainian World

Ukrainian doesn't just label words — it brings them to life. The earth is **земля́** — feminine, like a mother. Ukrainians say **земля́-ма́ти** (mother earth). The sun is **со́нце** — neuter, an impartial life-giver: **я́сне со́нце** (bright sun). The heart is **се́рце** — also neuter, belonging to everyone equally: **до́бре се́рце** (kind heart).

These aren't random assignments. They reflect how Ukrainian speakers see the world — and now you're starting to see it that way too.

### Your Competency Check

Can you identify the gender of these core words? Cover the answers and test yourself:

- **ім'я́** → ? (neuter — моє ім'я)
- **та́то** → ? (masculine — мій тато)
- **ма́ма** → ? (feminine — моя мама)
- **мі́сто** → ? (neuter — моє місто)

If you got all four right — including the tricky ones — great work! You've built a solid foundation for everything that comes next.
# Summary

In this module, you've learned the fundamental patterns of Ukrainian noun gender. Remember the color code: **Blue (Masculine)** for consonants, **Red (Feminine)** for -а/-я, and **Yellow (Neuter)** for -о/-е. While there are some tricky words like **тато** or **ім'я**, the 95% rule will serve you well. Keep practicing with **мій**, **моя**, and **моє** to build your intuition!

```

  words: 175

  - 'Cultural Hook: The Neuter Sun (сонце) — explain its role as an impartial, gentle life-giver in Ukrainian folklore, contrasting
    it with the masculine sun in Romance languages (el sol/le soleil).'
  - 'Visual Mnemonic Framework: Categorization logic using color codes — Blue for Masculine (Hard/Consonant), Red for Feminine
    (Open/A-ending), and Yellow for Neuter (Round/O-E-ending).'
- section: Презентація правил (Presentation of Rules)
  words: 300
  points:
  - 'Pattern Recognition for Endings: Masculine (consonant: стіл, хліб, дім), Feminine (-а/-я: книга, кімната, земля), and
    Neuter (-о/-е: вікно, місто, море).'
  - 'State Standard §4.2.2 Integration: Introduction of possessive pronouns ''мій, моя, моє'' as the primary diagnostic tool
    for gender agreement and identity.'
  - 'Syntactic Agreement: How gender dictates the form of adjectives and pronouns — examples with ''великий стіл'' (M), ''цікава
    книга'' (F), and ''чисте вікно'' (N).'
  - 'Identity and Family Dialogue: High-frequency context using ''брат'' (M), ''сестра'' (F), ''мама'' (F), and ''тато'' (M)
    to demonstrate natural vs. grammatical gender.'
- section: Практичні вправи (Practice Exercises)
  words: 300
  points:
  - 'Natural Gender Override Trap: The case of ''тато'' — explain that biological sex overrides the ''-o'' ending, making
    it Masculine (мій тато); contrast with ''місто'' (N).'
  - 'The Soft Sign Ambiguity (Ь): Drill with the high-frequency minimal pair ''день'' (Masculine) vs. ''ніч'' (Feminine);
    strategy for memorizing these essential ''soft'' exceptions.'
  - 'The ''Name'' Trap and Family 4: Explaining why ''ім''я'' (name) is Neuter despite ending in ''-я'' — contrast with Feminine
    ''земля'' to avoid common learner confusion.'
  - 'State Standard §4.2.1.1 Drill: Categorizing high-frequency nouns ''чоловік'' (man), ''жінка'' (woman), and ''місто''
    (city) into their respective gender buckets.'
- section: Самостійна робота (Independent Work/Production)
  words: 250
  points:
  - 'The ''It'' Trap Correction: Targeted drill to stop learners from using neuter ''воно'' for all objects; reinforcing ''стіл
    = він'' and ''книга = вона''.'
  - 'Mapping Gender to Modern Contexts: Using S.T.A.L.K.E.R. vocabulary — ''Артефакт'' (M), ''Зона'' (F), and ''Укриття''
    (N) as classification anchors.'
  - 'Applying Agreement: Creating simple descriptive phrases for personal items and nature terms using ''мій/моя/моє'' and
    basic adjectives (новий, цікавий, великий).'
- section: Культурний код та підсумок (Cultural Code and Summary)
  words: 175
  points:
  - 'Summary of gender prediction from endings: 95% predictability rule — reinforcement of the ''Soft Sign'' and ''Natural
    Gender'' exceptions.'
  - 'Cultural Reflection: How gendered language shapes the Ukrainian worldview — personification of nature (земля-мати, сонце-життя).'
  - 'Final Competency Check: Identification of gender for core identity vocabulary (ім''я, тато, мама, місто) as per State
    Standard requirements.'
vocabulary_hints:

  - брат (brother) — старший брат (older brother); high-frequency family term
  - сестра (sister) — молодша сестра (younger sister); high-frequency family term
  - мама (mother) — моя мама; люба мама; high-frequency feminine
  - тато (father) — мій тато (my dad); natural gender overrides -o ending; masculine trap
  - дім (house) — новий дім; рідний дім; high-frequency masculine
  - вікно (window) — відкрите вікно; велике вікно; чисте вікно; high-frequency neuter
  - книга (book) — цікава книга; стара книга; електронна книга; high-frequency feminine
  - місто (city) — моє місто; велике місто; State Standard §4.2.1.1 example
  recommended:
  - стіл (table) — дерев'яний стіл; великий стіл; новий стіл; core masculine
  - море (sea) — синє море; тепле море; high-frequency neuter
  - ніч (night) — добра ніч (good night); feminine exception with soft sign
  - день (day) — гарний день (good day); masculine contrast with 'ніч'
  - земля (earth) — рідна земля; чорна земля; feminine ending in -я
  - серце (heart) — добре серце; моє серце; high-frequency neuter
  - сонце (sun) — ясне сонце; cultural hook; neuter life-giver
  - собака (dog) — моя собака (my dog); feminine despite some colloquial masculine usage
  - ім'я (name) — моє повне ім'я; гарне ім'я; neuter exception (Family 4)
  - артефакт (artifact) — S.T.A.L.K.E.R. hook; masculine consonant ending

## Activities YAML

```yaml
- type: match-up
  title: Sort Nouns by Gender
  instruction: Match each Ukrainian noun to its grammatical gender.
  pairs:
  - left: стіл
    right: Masculine
  - left: книга
    right: Feminine
  - left: вікно
    right: Neuter
  - left: брат
    right: Masculine
  - left: земля
    right: Feminine
  - left: море
    right: Neuter
  - left: собака
    right: Feminine
  - left: серце
    right: Neuter
  - left: хліб
    right: Masculine
  - left: сестра
    right: Feminine
- type: quiz
  title: Identify Gender by Ending
  instruction: Choose the correct gender based on the word's ending.
  items:
  - question: What gender is дім (house)? It ends in a consonant.
    explanation: Nouns ending in a consonant are masculine.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: true
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is книга (book)? It ends in -а.
    explanation: Nouns ending in -а are feminine.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Feminine
      correct: true
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is вікно (window)? It ends in -о.
    explanation: Nouns ending in -о are neuter.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: true
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is тато (dad)? Be careful — it ends in -о but refers to
      a male person.
    explanation: Тато ends in -о, but because it refers to a male person, biological
      gender overrides the ending. It is masculine.
    options:
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: Masculine
      correct: true
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is море (sea)? It ends in -е.
    explanation: Nouns ending in -е are neuter.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: true
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is земля (earth)? It ends in -я.
    explanation: Nouns ending in -я are usually feminine.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Feminine
      correct: true
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is день (day)? It ends in a soft sign (ь).
    explanation: День is masculine. Soft sign nouns must be memorized — день is masculine,
      but ніч (night) is feminine.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: true
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is ім'я (name)? It ends in -я, but it is a special exception.
    explanation: Ім'я looks feminine because of -я, but it is actually neuter — моє
      ім'я.
    options:
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: true
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
- type: fill-in
  title: Choose the Correct Possessive
  instruction: Pick the right form of 'my' for each noun.
  items:
  - sentence: ___ стіл (table)
    answer: мій
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Стіл is masculine, so we use мій.
  - sentence: ___ книга (book)
    answer: моя
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Книга is feminine, so we use моя.
  - sentence: ___ вікно (window)
    answer: моє
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Вікно is neuter, so we use моє.
  - sentence: ___ тато (dad)
    answer: мій
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Тато refers to a male person, so it is masculine — мій тато.
  - sentence: ___ сестра (sister)
    answer: моя
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Сестра is feminine, so we use моя.
  - sentence: ___ місто (city)
    answer: моє
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Місто is neuter, so we use моє.
  - sentence: ___ брат (brother)
    answer: мій
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Брат is masculine, so we use мій.
  - sentence: ___ ім'я (name)
    answer: моє
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Ім'я is a neuter exception — моє ім'я.
- type: match-up
  title: Noun to Possessive Pronoun
  instruction: Match each noun with the correct possessive pronoun.
  pairs:
  - left: дім
    right: мій
  - left: мама
    right: моя
  - left: сонце
    right: моє
  - left: собака
    right: моя
  - left: артефакт
    right: мій
  - left: зона
    right: моя
  - left: укриття
    right: моє
  - left: серце
    right: моє
- type: group-sort
  title: Sort by Gender
  instruction: Place each noun into the correct gender group.
  groups:
  - name: Masculine (мій)
    items:
    - стіл
    - дім
    - брат
    - хліб
  - name: Feminine (моя)
    items:
    - книга
    - мама
    - сестра
    - земля
  - name: Neuter (моє)
    items:
    - вікно
    - місто
    - море
    - сонце
- type: true-false
  title: True or False?
  instruction: Decide whether each statement about Ukrainian gender is true or false.
  items:
  - statement: Nouns ending in a consonant are usually masculine.
    correct: true
    explanation: Correct! Consonant endings like стіл, дім, брат signal masculine
      gender.
  - statement: Nouns ending in -а or -я are always feminine.
    correct: false
    explanation: Usually yes, but there are exceptions. Ім'я ends in -я but is neuter.
  - statement: The word тато is neuter because it ends in -о.
    correct: false
    explanation: Тато refers to a male person, so biological gender overrides the
      ending — it is masculine.
  - statement: Моя is the feminine form of 'my' in Ukrainian.
    correct: true
    explanation: Correct! Мій (masculine), моя (feminine), моє (neuter).
  - statement: The word ніч (night) is masculine.
    correct: false
    explanation: Ніч is feminine — добра ніч (good night). Soft sign nouns need to
      be memorized.
  - statement: Вікно is neuter because it ends in -о.
    correct: true
    explanation: Correct! The -о ending signals neuter gender.
  - statement: In Ukrainian, every noun has a grammatical gender.
    correct: true
    explanation: Correct! Ukrainian has three genders — masculine, feminine, and neuter
      — for all nouns.
  - statement: The word день (day) is feminine.
    correct: false
    explanation: День is masculine — мій день, гарний день.
- type: unjumble
  title: Put the Words in Order
  instruction: Arrange the words to form a correct Ukrainian phrase.
  items:
  - words:
    - стіл
    - Це
    - мій
    answer: Це мій стіл
  - words:
    - книга
    - моя
    - Це
    answer: Це моя книга
  - words:
    - вікно
    - моє
    - Це
    answer: Це моє вікно
  - words:
    - брат
    - мій
    - Це
    answer: Це мій брат
  - words:
    - сестра
    - моя
    - Це
    answer: Це моя сестра
  - words:
    - місто
    - моє
    - Це
    answer: Це моє місто
- type: anagram
  title: Unscramble the Word
  instruction: Rearrange the letters to form a Ukrainian word from the lesson.
  items:
  - scrambled: г а н и к
    answer: книга
  - scrambled: і с т л
    answer: стіл
  - scrambled: о н і к в
    answer: вікно
  - scrambled: р е м о
    answer: море
  - scrambled: р а б т
    answer: брат
  - scrambled: ц е р с е
    answer: серце

```

---

## Research Notes

# Дослідження: The Gender Code

## State Standard Reference
§4.2.1.1: "Noun case form formation — singular/plural, all 4 declensions basic" (lines 603–631)
§4.2.2: "Pronoun gender and number forms" (lines 659–667)
Alignment: This module introduces the three-gender system as the gateway to §4.2.1.1 (declension families) and deploys possessive pronouns мій/моя/моє as the §4.2.2-required diagnostic tool for gender identification. All four declension families are previewed at recognition level.

---

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Gender | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|--------|-------|------------------|
| брат | M | High-frequency family; hard consonant ending model | старший брат, мій брат |
| сестра | F | High-frequency family; canonical -а ending | молодша сестра, моя сестра |
| мама | F | Ultra-high-frequency; canonical -а ending | люба мама, моя мама |
| тато | M | Natural gender trap; -о ending but biologically M | мій тато (NOT моє тато) |
| дім | M | Core masculine; hard consonant ending | новий дім, рідний дім |
| вікно | N | Canonical neuter; -о ending model | велике вікно, чисте вікно |
| книга | F | Ultra-high-frequency; canonical -а ending | цікава книга, стара книга |
| місто | N | State Standard §4.2.1.1 exemplar; neuter -о | велике місто, моє місто |
| стіл | M | Core masculine; hard consonant ending | великий стіл, новий стіл |
| море | N | High-frequency neuter; -е ending variant | синє море, тепле море |
| день | M | Soft sign masculine — critical minimal pair | гарний день |
| ніч | F | Soft sign feminine — critical minimal pair | добра ніч |
| земля | F | Canonical -я ending feminine | рідна земля, чорна земля |
| серце | N | High-frequency neuter; -е ending | добре серце, моє серце |
| сонце | N | Cultural hook; neuter life-giver | ясне сонце |
| ім'я | N | Family 4 exception; -я ending but N not F | моє повне ім'я, гарне ім'я |
| артефакт | M | S.T.A.L.K.E.R. hook; hard consonant ending | рідкісний артефакт |
| зона | F | S.T.A.L.K.E.R. hook; canonical -а ending | небезпечна зона |
| укриття | N | S.T.A.L.K.E.R. hook; neuter -я (decl. family 4) | надійне укриття |

---

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Земля-мати (Mother Earth)** — Ukrainian personifies earth as feminine: «рідна земля», «чорна земля». This is not arbitrary — the -а/-я ending IS the feminine marker. Land, mother, and the -а ending form a single cultural-linguistic cluster learners can anchor to.

2. **Сонце as neuter** — Unlike French (le soleil, M) and Spanish (el sol, M), the Ukrainian sun is neuter — an impartial, life-giving force, not a gendered deity. This contrast with Romance languages is memorable for English speakers who have studied other languages, and grounds the "neuter = neither" concept.

3. **S.T.A.L.K.E.R. vocabulary as classification anchors** — The Ukrainian-developed game franchise provides three perfectly calibrated exemplars: Артефакт (М, hard consonant), Зона (Ж, -а ending), Укриття (С, -я/neuter). Using culturally resonant Ukrainian content as a mnemonic is more effective than abstract paradigm tables.

---

## Common Learner Errors

1. **"It" transfer** — English speakers default to a single neuter pronoun for all objects. Ukrainian requires він for стіл, вона for книга, воно for вікно. → Drill: replace the noun with він/вона/воно; if the learner always writes воно, the "it" trap has fired.

2. **The -о exception (тато vs місто)** — Learners apply the neuter rule (-о → N) to тато, yielding «моє тато» instead of «мій тато». Natural gender of animate nouns overrides morphological gender. → Contrastive minimal pair drill: тато (M) vs місто (N), серце (N) vs тато (M).

3. **Soft sign ambiguity (день vs ніч)** — No reliable rule predicts soft-sign gender. Learners must memorize these as lexical items. → Textbook pattern (Grade 3, Vашуленко-style): pair them in antonym exercises — «день — ніч» forces learners to store both simultaneously with their gender tags.

4. **ім'я treated as feminine** — The -я ending looks like земля (F), but ім'я is neuter (Family 4: plural ім'я → імена). → Contrastive pair: «моя земля» vs «моє ім'я» — same ending, different gender, distinguished by pronoun.

---

## Cross-References

- Builds on: a1-02 (The Cyrillic Code II) — letter recognition needed to read endings; foundational literacy prerequisite
- Prepares for: a1-09 (This Is, I Am) — gender agreement with «це є», «я є»; a1-26 (Adjectives) — full adjective-noun gender agreement requires this foundation

---

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise**: Present three columns of items without labels — a photo of a дім, книга, вікно — and their pronoun substitutions: він, вона, воно. Ask learners to group the nouns under the correct pronoun by examining the word ending. Learners discover the ending→pronoun pattern before the rule is stated. Modeled on Vашуленко Grade 3 antonym/synonym pairing exercises (Grade 3, p.2 exercise conventions).

- **Best context**: A classroom/home environment — labeling objects in the room ("Це стіл. Він новий." / "Це книга. Вона цікава.") using the Це + noun pattern permitted in the pre-verbal phase. No verbs needed; мій/моя/моє + noun is sufficient.

- **Contrastive pairs**: (1) тато vs місто — same -о ending, different genders; (2) день vs ніч — both soft-sign endings, different genders; (3) земля vs ім'я — both -я endings, different families (F vs N).

---

## Notes for Content Writing

- Module is in the pre-verbal phase (M7 < M15): ALL classroom instructions stay in English; bilingual headers required (e.g., «## Рід іменника — Noun Gender»).
- Possessive pronouns мій/моя/моє are allowed as diagnostic tools per §4.2.2 — they are pre-taught diagnostic forms, not full pronoun conjugation.
- The color-code mnemonic (Blue=M, Red=F, Yellow=N) from the plan should appear in a visual reference table early and referenced throughout.
- S.T.A.L.K.E.R. hook works best in the Practice section, not Introduction — learners need the rule first, then the culturally engaging application.
- Keep «собака» in the recommended list but do NOT make it a focal example — the M/F ambiguity is a distractor at this level.
- Max 10 words per Ukrainian sentence; all Ukrainian examples go in tables, bullet lists, or blockquote boxes — no Ukrainian in English paragraphs except bolded inline words.


## Resource Discovery

### Videos
- [All About Ukrainian Instrumental Case: Grammar, Usage, Practice](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7q87c9T2QeA) (Let's Learn Ukrainian) — The video primarily explains the Ukrainian Instrumental Case and its usage, not directly the concept of noun gender, which is the module's core topic. It uses gendered nouns as examples within the context of case declension, offering a tangential connection.

### Blog Articles
- [Dobra Forma: Gender of Nouns](https://opentext.ku.edu/dobraforma/chapter/1-1/) (dobraforma)
- [Dobra Forma: Gender of Nouns (Masculine and Feminine)](https://opentext.ku.edu/dobraforma/chapter/1-2/) (dobraforma)
- [Dobra Forma: Gender of Nouns (Neuter)](https://opentext.ku.edu/dobraforma/chapter/1-3/) (dobraforma)
- [Ukrainian Lessons Podcast Season 4](https://www.ukrainianlessons.com/episode157/) (ukrainianlessons.com)
- [Noun Genders in Ukrainian](https://www.ukrainianlessons.com/noun-genders-in-ukrainian/) (ukrainianlessons.com)

## Friction Constraints (Past Review Findings)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.

---

## Your Task: Plan Adherence + Factual Verification

### PART A: Plan Adherence Check (MANDATORY)

The plan YAML above is the source of truth for what the module MUST contain. Check EVERY point systematically:

#### A1. Content Outline Points

For EACH section in `content_outline`, and for EACH `points` entry within that section:
1. Search the module content for evidence that this point is addressed
2. Classify as:
   - **COVERED** — the point is clearly addressed in the content. Quote the relevant sentence(s).
   - **MISSING** — the point does not appear in the content. This is a **MEDIUM** severity issue.
   - **PARTIAL** — some aspect is covered but key details are missing. Note what's missing.

**Output a checklist:**
```
### Plan Point Coverage
- Section "Вступ — Introduction":
  - [x] Point 1: "A1 immersion 10-50%..." — COVERED (line ~5: "...")
  - [ ] Point 2: "Cultural hook: Cyrillic script..." — MISSING
  ...
```

#### A2. Required Vocabulary

For EACH word in `vocabulary_hints.required`:
1. Search the module content (not just the word list section — the entire prose)
2. Check if it appears in the activities YAML (if provided in the content above)
3. Classify as:
   - **PRESENT** — found in prose AND activities
   - **PROSE ONLY** — in prose but not in any activity (LOW)
   - **MISSING** — not in prose at all (**HIGH** severity)

#### A3. Activity Hints

For EACH entry in `activity_hints`:
1. Check if an activity of that `type` exists
2. Check if the `focus` matches
3. Check if the `items` count is met
4. Flag missing activity types as **MEDIUM**

#### A4. Section Word Budgets

For EACH section in `content_outline` with a `words` target:
1. Estimate the word count of the corresponding content section
2. Flag sections >10% under their target as **MEDIUM**

#### A5. Pronunciation Videos (if plan has `pronunciation_videos`)

Check that video links from the plan are embedded in the content. Flag missing embeds as **MEDIUM**.

---

### PART B: Factual Verification

For EVERY grammar explanation, factual claim, cultural assertion, and historical reference in the module content:

1. **Search the reference sources above** for confirming or contradicting evidence
2. **Classify each claim** as one of:
   - **CONFIRMED [Tier 1]** — matches a reference source. Cite the source (chunk ID or source identifier) and quote both the module claim and the reference.
   - **DISCREPANCY [Tier 1]** — contradicts a reference source. HIGH severity. Cite both the module claim and the contradicting reference with chunk ID. Suggest the correct version.
   - **UNVERIFIED** — no reference coverage. Flag explicitly. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge. Do NOT mark as correct or incorrect — just "UNVERIFIED".

### Citation Enforcement

Every DISCREPANCY entry MUST include:
- Exact quote from the module content
- Exact quote from the reference source
- **Chunk ID or source identifier** (e.g., "Textbook, Grade 3, chunk_abc123" or "VESUM: lemma X")

Free-text citations with no chunk ID or source identifier carry ZERO weight and will be programmatically discarded.

### Special Checks

1. **Callout boxes** (`[!did-you-know]`, `[!myth-buster]`, `[!culture-note]`, `[!fun-fact]`): These are the #1 source of fabrication. Scrutinize every claim.
2. **Grammar rules**: Verify rule statements against textbook references. Flag overgeneralizations.
3. **Etymologies**: Verify word origins against references. Flag folk etymologies.
4. **Statistics and dates**: Flag unsourced numbers.
5. **VESUM failures**: Words not found in VESUM may be misspellings or Russianisms.

---

## Output Format

You MUST use these exact delimiters:

```
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_START===
## Plan Adherence Summary

**Content outline points:** N total, N covered, N missing, N partial
**Required vocabulary:** N total, N present, N missing
**Activity hints:** N total, N matched, N missing
**Plan Adherence Score:** X/10
(10 = all points covered; deduct 1 per MISSING content point, 2 per MISSING required word)

### Plan Point Coverage

[Checklist from Part A1 — every point with [x] or [ ] and evidence]

### Required Vocabulary Coverage

| Word | In Prose? | In Activities? | Status |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| ... | YES/NO | YES/NO | PRESENT / PROSE ONLY / MISSING |

### Activity Hints Coverage

| Hint Type | Focus | Required Items | Found? | Actual Items |
|-----------|-------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| ... | ... | N | YES/NO | N |

### Missing Plan Points (Fix Targets)

[List each MISSING or PARTIAL point with the exact plan text and which section it belongs to.
These become fix targets in the SECTION_FIX block below.]

---

## Factual Verification Summary

**Total claims checked:** N
**Confirmed [Tier 1]:** N
**Discrepancies [Tier 1]:** N
**Unverified:** N
**Factual Alignment Score:** X/10

## Confirmed Claims

[List confirmed claims with source citations]

## Discrepancies

### Discrepancy 1: [brief title]
- **Module says:** "[exact quote from module]"
- **Reference says:** "[exact quote from reference source]"
- **Source:** [chunk ID / source identifier]
- **Severity:** HIGH
- **Suggested fix:** [what to change]

[... more discrepancies ...]

## Unverified Claims

[List claims with no reference coverage]

## Verdict

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
(FAIL if any HIGH severity discrepancy OR 3+ MISSING plan points found)
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_END===
```

If you find fixable discrepancies, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:

```
===SECTION_FIX_START===
FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/the-gender-code.md
FIND:
[exact text from module with the error]
REPLACE:
[corrected text based on reference source]
---
[... more pairs, max 15 ...]
===SECTION_FIX_END===
```

---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT evaluate style, naturalness, or pedagogy — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT score dimensions (except Plan Adherence and Factual Alignment) — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT evaluate factual claims from your own knowledge — ONLY compare against provided references
- Plan adherence checking uses the plan YAML as ground truth — this is objective, not subjective
- Do NOT fabricate references — if no reference covers a claim, mark it UNVERIFIED
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs (prioritize MISSING plan points over minor factual fixes)
